system_prompt: You are a senior web strategist at a digital agency specializing in SEO, conversion optimization, content strategy, and UX for marketing websites. You evaluate pages the way an agency would before a client proposal — looking for concrete wins, not theoretical advice. You are precise, analytical, and data-driven. ## Analysis Constraints 1. Analyze ONLY using the provided metrics and content — do not use external knowledge about the specific website. 2. You MUST reference specific metrics by name and value in every finding (e.g., "Word count of 300 is below the 600-word SEO threshold" or "4 out of 5 images (80%%) lack alt text"). 3. Check for Heading Hierarchy Violations: H3 before any H2, missing H1, multiple H1s, level skips (H1 → H3). 4. Evaluate meta title length (ideal: 50–60 chars) and meta description length (ideal: 120–160 chars) using the provided character counts. 5. Consider rich visual media holistically: a page may lack traditional tags but use SVGs, CSS animations, video embeds, Lottie animations, canvas/WebGL, or 3D elements. Do NOT penalize visual quality if rich media alternatives are present. 6. Assess technical SEO signals: viewport meta, canonical URL, robots meta, Open Graph tags, Twitter Card, and structured data (JSON-LD) presence. 7. Evaluate CTA quality: are there enough CTAs for the page length? Are they above the fold? Is the language action-oriented? 8. For content depth: consider topical coverage, use of subheadings to organize content, internal/external link strategy, and whether the content matches likely search intent. 9. Do NOT make generic statements — every claim must cite a metric or content excerpt. 10. Scores must be integers from 1 to 10. ## Recommendation Constraints 11. Generate 5 recommendations in the "recommendations" array. Only reduce to 4 if the page is near-perfect in one category, or 3 if the page excels in multiple areas. Default to 5. 12. Every recommendation MUST be directly grounded in your analysis findings — never generic advice. 13. Prioritize by impact: priority 1 = highest impact, must-fix; priority 2 = significant improvement; priority 3 = nice-to-have optimization. 14. The "grounded_metric" field must cite the exact data point (e.g., "4 images missing alt text", "Meta title is 78 chars — exceeds 60-char ideal"). 15. The "action" field must be a specific, implementable step — not vague advice. 16. The "expected_impact" field must describe the concrete expected outcome. 17. Focus recommendations on the lowest-scoring categories first. ## Quality Guards 18. THINK BEFORE SCORING: For each category, first identify the relevant metrics, then compare against best practices, then assess real-world impact — only then assign a score. Never pick a score first and justify it after. 19. ANTI-HALLUCINATION: If the extracted metrics say a feature is MISSING (e.g., "Structured Data (JSON-LD): None" or "Canonical URL: MISSING"), treat it as definitively absent. Do not infer its presence from the page text. 20. NO DUPLICATE RECOMMENDATIONS: Each of the 5 recommendations must address a distinct issue. Do not suggest the same fix in different wording. 21. PROFESSIONAL TONE: Avoid vague marketing buzzwords ("leverage", "supercharge", "synergize"). Write like a senior consultant delivering a client audit report. - 9-10: Exceptional — best practices exceeded, clear competitive advantage - 7-8: Good — solid foundation with minor improvements possible - 5-6: Average — functional but missing key optimizations that impact rankings/conversions - 3-4: Below average — significant gaps that hurt performance - 1-2: Critical — fundamental issues that need immediate attention Return a single JSON object with: 1. Scores and detailed analysis for each of the 5 categories (structure, messaging, CTAs, content depth, UX) 2. A "recommendations" array with 5 prioritized, actionable recommendations grounded in your analysis (reduce to 4 or 3 only if the page truly excels) All in one structured response matching the provided schema. ## Good vs. Bad — calibrate your output quality BAD finding (vague, no data): "The content is too short and doesn't explain the product well." GOOD finding (grounded, specific): "Word count of 300 is below the 600-word threshold for competitive mid-funnel queries, and with no rich media alternatives (0 videos, 0 canvas elements), the page lacks depth to rank." BAD recommendation (generic): "Improve SEO by adding alt text to images." GOOD recommendation (actionable, cited): "Add descriptive alt text to the 4 images missing it (80%% of total). Prioritize the hero banner and product thumbnails, as these appear in Google Image search results."
user_prompt: ## Extracted Page Metrics (Deterministic — scraped from HTML) ### Content Metrics - Word Count: 384 - CTA Count: 25 - Internal Links: 49 - External Links: 2 ### Image & Visual Media - Total Images (): 53 - Images Missing Alt Text: 0 (0.0%%) - Images with Decorative Alt (alt=""): 51 - Rich Visual Media: 2 SVG elements, Video embeds detected, CSS animations/transitions detected, WebGL/3D elements detected ### Technical SEO - Meta Title: B2B Digital Agency for Enterprises | eight25 (44 chars — ideal: 50-60) - Meta Description: Global B2B digital agency offering enterprise web design, mobile apps, and strategic consulting for Fortune 500 firms. (118 chars — ideal: 120-160) - Viewport Meta: Present - Canonical URL: Present - Robots Meta: Present - Open Graph Tags: Present - Twitter Card: Present - Structured Data (JSON-LD): None ### Heading Structure - Heading Counts: {"h1": 1, "h2": 5, "h3": 10, "h4": 0, "h5": 0, "h6": 0} ## Heading Hierarchy (order as found in HTML) [["H1", "Digital experiences for the modern enterprise"], ["H2", "The world’s best companies"], ["H2", "choose to work with eight25"], ["H3", "Google"], ["H3", "Qlik"], ["H3", "Intralinks"], ["H3", "Andela"], ["H3", "Qualcomm"], ["H2", "Insights"], ["H3", "Analyzing the ‘Request a Demo’ Page: Unpacking Why Conversions Are Lagging"], ["H3", "Uncovering Homepage Hero Friction: Why Visitors Bounce and How to Fix It"], ["H3", "Why Is Brand Alignment Important For B2B Enterprise Websites?"], ["H3", "Assessment:  Is your website working hard enough?"]] ## Page Content (visible text excerpt) Digital experiences for the modern enterprise We build brands and web experiences that help you build the ultimate customer journey. Learn More The world’s best companies choose to work with eight25 Google Qlik Intralinks Andela Qualcomm Insights Analyzing the ‘Request a Demo’ Page: Unpacking Why Conversions Are Lagging Read More Uncovering Homepage Hero Friction: Why Visitors Bounce and How to Fix It Read More Why Is Brand Alignment Important For B2B Enterprise Websites? Read More Assessment:  Is your website working hard enough? We’ve been privileged to work with Fortune 500 companies and funded startups alike, to build some of the most innovative solutions on the web. Review an in-depth assessment analyzing the strengths, weaknesses, and opportunities of your digital presence. Success stories… Let’s talk Name Email Phone Message Submit Perform a complete website audit in two parts: **STEP 0 — Page Classification**: First, determine the primary purpose of this page from its content, CTAs, and structure (e.g., SaaS landing page, e-commerce product page, blog/article, documentation, portfolio, corporate homepage). Tailor your scoring expectations to that page type — a blog post needs fewer CTAs than a landing page, and a portfolio site may rely on visuals over word count. **PART 1 — Analysis**: Evaluate the page across all five categories: - **Structure & SEO**: Heading hierarchy (H1 presence, proper nesting, no skips), meta tag quality (title/description length and relevance), technical SEO signals (viewport, canonical, OG tags, structured data), and overall HTML organization. - **Messaging & Clarity**: Value proposition clarity, brand voice consistency, whether the content answers the user's likely intent, and if key messages are prioritized above the fold. - **CTAs**: Quantity relative to page length, action-oriented language quality, variety of CTAs (primary vs secondary), and strategic placement signals. - **Content Depth**: Word count relative to topic complexity, use of subheadings for scannability, internal/external link strategy for topical authority, and whether the content demonstrates expertise (E-E-A-T signals). - **UX**: Content readability structure (short paragraphs, logical flow), mobile-readiness signals (viewport meta), image optimization (alt text), rich media usage for engagement, and overall page organization. For each category, provide a score (1-10), findings grounded in specific metrics, and direct evidence. **PART 2 — Recommendations**: Based on your analysis above, generate 5 prioritized recommendations (reduce to 4 or 3 only if the page truly excels in most categories). Focus on the lowest-scoring categories first. Each recommendation must cite specific data and be the kind a web agency would include in a client audit report. Return everything as a single structured JSON object matching the schema.

structure_analysis.findings: The page exhibits a well-organized heading hierarchy with a single H1 and proper nesting of H2s and H3s, avoiding any level skips. Meta title (44 chars) and meta description (118 chars) are concise and within optimal length ranges. Essential technical SEO elements like viewport meta, canonical URL, robots meta, Open Graph tags, and Twitter Card are all present. However, a significant technical gap is the complete absence of Structured Data (JSON-LD). Furthermore, while 0 images are missing alt text, 51 out of 53 images (96.2%%) have empty alt attributes (alt=""), which is a critical accessibility and SEO oversight.
structure_analysis.evidence: Meta Title: 'B2B Digital Agency for Enterprises | eight25' (44 chars). Meta Description: 'Global B2B digital agency offering enterprise web design, mobile apps, and strategic consulting for Fortune 500 firms.' (118 chars). Heading Counts: {"h1": 1, "h2": 5, "h3": 10}. Heading Hierarchy: H1 -> H2 -> H3. Structured Data (JSON-LD): None. Images with Decorative Alt (alt=""): 51.

messaging_analysis.findings: The page clearly articulates its value proposition, 'Digital experiences for the modern enterprise. We build brands and web experiences that help you build the ultimate customer journey,' prominently above the fold. The target audience of 'enterprises' and 'Fortune 500 firms' is well-defined. Strong social proof is provided through client logos (Google, Qlik, Qualcomm) and a dedicated 'Success stories…' section. Service offerings are clearly outlined in the navigation and implied through content sections like 'Insights'. The language is professional and directly addresses the likely search intent of B2B clients seeking digital agency services.
messaging_analysis.evidence: H1: 'Digital experiences for the modern enterprise'. Sub-headline: 'We build brands and web experiences that help you build the ultimate customer journey.' Section: 'The world's best companies choose to work with eight25' followed by client names. Meta Description: 'Global B2B digital agency offering enterprise web design, mobile apps, and strategic consulting for Fortune 500 firms.'

cta_analysis.findings: The page features 25 CTAs for a word count of 384, indicating a high density that could lead to decision fatigue. Key CTAs like 'Let's talk' (in navigation) and 'View all work' are strategically placed above the fold. Other action-oriented CTAs such as 'Read More' for blog posts and 'Watch Video' for success stories are present mid-page. A contact form with a 'Submit' button serves as a primary conversion point at the bottom. While the language is action-oriented and variety exists, the sheer volume of clickable elements categorized as CTAs may dilute the impact of primary conversion goals.
cta_analysis.evidence: CTA Count: 25. Word Count: 384. CTAs observed: 'Let's talk', 'View all work', 'Read More', 'Watch Video', 'Submit' (for contact form).

content_depth_analysis.findings: With a word count of 384, the textual content is relatively low for a corporate homepage targeting enterprise clients, which typically requires more detailed explanations of services and methodologies. However, this is partially compensated by the effective use of rich visual media, including 2 SVG elements, video embeds, CSS animations, and WebGL/3D elements. The content covers a good range of topics, from brand and creative services to technology and growth, organized by clear subheadings. The page demonstrates E-E-A-T through client testimonials, success stories, and blog excerpts, and features 49 internal links to guide users to deeper content.
content_depth_analysis.evidence: Word Count: 384. Rich Visual Media: 2 SVG elements, Video embeds detected, CSS animations/transitions detected, WebGL/3D elements detected. Internal Links: 49. Headings like 'Insights' and 'Success stories…' organize content.

ux_analysis.findings: The page offers a visually engaging experience with extensive rich media, including video embeds for success stories and various animations, which enhances user engagement. Content is well-structured with short paragraphs and clear subheadings, improving readability and scannability. The presence of a viewport meta tag indicates mobile responsiveness. However, a critical UX flaw is that 51 out of 53 images (96.2%%) have empty alt text. This significantly hinders accessibility for users relying on screen readers, as these images convey no descriptive information, creating a barrier to understanding the page content.
ux_analysis.evidence: Rich Visual Media: 2 SVG elements, Video embeds detected, CSS animations/transitions detected, WebGL/3D elements detected. Viewport Meta: Present. Images with Decorative Alt (alt=""): 51.

recommendations.title: Implement Descriptive Alt Text for Images
recommendations.description: A significant number of images (51 out of 53) have empty alt attributes. While not 'missing' alt text, this means screen readers and search engines cannot interpret the image content, negatively impacting accessibility and image SEO.
recommendations.action: Review all 51 images with empty alt text. For images that convey meaningful information (e.g., client logos, service visuals, case study thumbnails), add concise, descriptive alt text. For purely decorative images, ensure they are handled appropriately (e.g., via CSS background images or ARIA roles if they must remain as tags).
recommendations.expected_impact: Significantly improve accessibility for users with visual impairments, enhance image search visibility, and provide search engines with better context about the page's visual content.

recommendations.title: Add Structured Data (JSON-LD)
recommendations.description: The page currently lacks any structured data implementation. For an agency website, structured data can help search engines better understand the organization, its services, and potentially enable rich snippets in search results.
recommendations.action: Implement Organization schema, Service schema, and potentially VideoObject schema (for the success stories) using JSON-LD. Ensure all required properties are accurately populated.
recommendations.expected_impact: Increase the likelihood of appearing in rich search results (e.g., knowledge panels, carousels), improve search engine understanding of the agency's offerings, and potentially boost organic click-through rates.

recommendations.title: Optimize CTA Density and Clarity
recommendations.description: With 25 CTAs for only 384 words, the page presents a high density of calls to action. This can overwhelm users, making it difficult to identify the primary conversion paths and potentially leading to decision paralysis.
recommendations.action: Conduct an audit of all 25 elements currently counted as CTAs. Clearly differentiate between primary conversion-focused CTAs (e.g., 'Let's Talk', 'Request a Proposal') and secondary navigational links. Reduce the visual prominence or consolidate secondary CTAs to ensure primary actions stand out more effectively.
recommendations.expected_impact: Improve user focus on key conversion goals, reduce cognitive load, and increase the conversion rate for high-value actions by making them more prominent and less diluted.

recommendations.title: Expand Core Service Content Depth
recommendations.description: The current word count of 384, while supplemented by rich media, is relatively low for an enterprise-focused agency homepage. This limits the opportunity to provide in-depth explanations of complex services, methodologies, and unique value propositions, which are crucial for B2B decision-makers.
recommendations.action: Strategically expand key content sections, particularly those detailing services or success stories, to provide more comprehensive information. Aim to increase the word count to at least 600-800 words, focusing on elaborating on 'how' the agency delivers results and the specific benefits for enterprise clients.
recommendations.expected_impact: Enhance the page's topical authority and expertise, provide more valuable information for potential clients, improve organic search rankings for relevant long-tail keywords, and increase user engagement by offering deeper insights.

recommendations.title: Integrate Textual Client Testimonials
recommendations.description: While the page effectively uses client logos and video success stories for social proof, integrating concise textual testimonials could provide immediate, scannable credibility without requiring video playback, further reinforcing trust.
recommendations.grounded_metric: Presence of 'The world's best companies choose to work with eight25' and 'Success stories…' sections.
recommendations.action: Add short, impactful client quotes (1-2 sentences) near the client logos section or alongside the video success stories. These should highlight specific benefits or positive experiences clients have had with eight25.
recommendations.expected_impact: Instantly build greater trust and credibility with new visitors, provide quick and scannable proof points of the agency's value, and reinforce the messaging without additional user effort.
